mnemonics.title: CLASS - Types of System Programs
mnemonics.content: CLASS stands for: C-Compilers & Interpreters, L-Loaders & Linkers, A-Assemblers, S-System Management Software, S-Storage & File Management. These are the essential system programs that form the backbone of OS functionality.

mnemonics.title: M.A.L.E. - OS Architecture Types
mnemonics.content: M.A.L.E. = Monolithic (simple, all in kernel), Layered (hierarchical structure), Exokernel (minimal core). Add 'Micro' for Microkernel (minimal services in kernel, rest in user space).

mnemonics.title: BOOT - System Startup Sequence
mnemonics.content: BOOT = Basic Input/Output System loads → Operating system kernel → Device drivers → User space programs. Remember: BIOS/UEFI POST (Power-On Self-Test) → Bootloader → Kernel → Init process.

mnemonics.title: KISS - Kernel Functions
mnemonics.content: KISS = Keep It Simple Stupid - Kernel manages: Process Scheduling, Memory Management, File System, Device Management, Security & Protection. These are the core OS responsibilities.

mnemonics.title: DICE - System Call Types
mnemonics.content: DICE = Device control, Information maintenance, File maintenance, Process control, Communication. These are the five major categories of system calls.

acronyms.usage: Firmware that initializes hardware during boot process; first software that runs when computer is turned on.
Modern replacement for BIOS with faster boot times, better security, and support for larger drives.
Text-based interface where users type commands (e.g., Terminal, Command Prompt); used in system programming for direct OS interaction.
Visual interface with icons, windows, menus; built on top of system programs for user-friendly interaction.
Set of system calls that allow applications to request OS services; system programs provide APIs for other software.
Standard API for Unix-like systems; ensures system programs work across different OS implementations.
Visual interface with windows, icons, menus; provides system program access through point-and-click.
Set of definitions and protocols for building application software; system programs expose APIs for programmers.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine an ice cream sundae or wedding cake: Hardware at the bottom (foundation), then kernel (core ice cream), then system programs (sprinkles/sauce), then user interface (top cherry). Each layer rests on the one below it.
Picture a single giant factory building where ALL production (process management, memory, file system, drivers) happens under ONE roof - fast but chaotic if something breaks.

Think of a small central hub (like a train station) with separate small buildings around it - each handles specific tasks (file service, memory service). They communicate through the central hub. Easier to fix but slightly slower.

Imagine a carpenter's workshop: OS is the workshop building, kernel is the workshop manager, and system programs (compilers, editors, loaders) are the individual tools (hammer, saw, drill) that workers (applications) use.

Think of waking up: BIOS is the alarm clock (initial check), bootloader is your alarm going off and getting up, kernel is you becoming fully awake and aware, and user programs are you starting your daily activities.

Picture a shopping mall: Has a central management office (microkernel-like core) but also has many specialized in-store departments (monolithic-style modules) - combining benefits of both architectures.

analogies.analogy: Just as a government provides essential services (defense, roads, courts) to citizens, the OS provides essential services (memory, CPU time, file access) to programs. System programs are like government departments (DMV, tax office) that handle specific tasks.

The kernel is like the brain of the OS - it controls everything, manages resources, and coordinates all activities. Just as your brain controls your body without you being consciously aware, the kernel runs quietly in the background.

System calls act like a hotel receptionist - applications (guests) cannot directly access hotel resources (hardware), so they ask the receptionist (system call) to handle requests (open file, allocate memory, create process).

Device drivers are like translators in a meeting: the OS speaks one language, hardware speaks another. Drivers translate between them so they can communicate - without drivers, hardware and software cannot understand each other.
The loader is like a project manager who brings a new employee (program) into the office, gives them a desk (memory space), and ensures they have all needed resources to start working.

The file system is like a library organization: the librarian (file system) knows where every book (file) is stored, maintains the catalog (directory structure), and ensures only authorized people can access certain sections (file permissions).
